title: The Sequence Modeling Recap
concept: We have covered text representation (Bag of Words, TF-IDF), learned embeddings (Word2Vec, FastText), recurrent models (RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs), and sequence tasks.

exercises.prompt: What kind of data are sequence models best suited for?

options.text: Data where the order of elements matters (like text or speech)

exercises.explain: Sequence models are designed for data where the order of items matters, such as text, speech audio, or music.

cards.title: The Journey of Words
cards.body: We've traveled a long way! From counting words like beans in a jar to building complex neural networks that can 'remember' the past. Let's take a quick look back at how we taught AI to understand the flow of language.

cards.title: What is Sequence Modeling?
cards.body: Sequence modeling is the art of teaching computers to understand data that comes in a specific order, like words in a sentence or notes in a song. Over time, we've moved from simply counting words to using smart neural networks that remember context and order.

cards.title: The Library Tour
cards.body: Imagine sorting books in a library. Bag of Words just counts the words like stacking all books together without order. RNNs are like reading a book page-by-page, keeping the plot in your head as you go. Transformers are like having a magical scanner that looks at all pages at once, instantly highlighting how different chapters connect!

cards.title: Sequence Models in Action
cards.body: Think of a text prediction app on your phone. If you type 'I want to eat a...', a simple model might suggest 'the' because it's a common word. A sequence model, however, remembers 'eat' and suggests 'pizza' or 'apple' instead!

cards.title: Is New Always Better?
cards.body: Many think that because newer models (like Transformers) exist, older tools like TF-IDF or Word2Vec are completely useless. In reality, simple methods are still used every day because they are incredibly fast, lightweight, and perfect for simple search tasks!

cards.title: Counting vs. Flow
cards.body: Let's compare the traditional counting approach with modern sequence modeling.

left.text: Counts words (TF-IDF). Fast, but loses the order and deep meaning of sentences.

right.text: Tracks order (RNN/LSTM). Understands context, flow, and long-distance relationships.

exercises.prompt: Which model was specifically designed to solve the problem of forgetting things in long sentences?
exercises.explain: LSTMs introduced special gates (like the forget gate) to help the network decide what information to keep or throw away over long sequences.

exercises.prompt: True or False: RNNs process all words in a sentence at the exact same time.
exercises.explain: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process inputs step-by-step in order, which is why they are slower at handling very long sequences compared to parallel models.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to highlight the key difference between Bag of Words and recurrent models like LSTMs.
exercises.sentence: Bag of Words only counts word frequencies, but recurrent models pay attention to word order.
exercises.explain: Bag of Words completely ignores the order of words, whereas LSTMs and GRUs are designed specifically to track sequence order.

exercises.prompt: Find the correct statement about word embeddings:

options.text: Word2Vec changes the meaning of a word depending on the sentence it is in.
Word2Vec gives each word a fixed numerical vector, regardless of context.
Bag of Words is a type of learned word embedding.

exercises.explain: Response B is correct because Word2Vec generates fixed embeddings for words, while contextual models change the embedding depending on the surrounding words.

exercises.prompt: As we look back at the history of sequence models, what is the biggest change in how they process text?

options.text: We no longer need to convert text into numbers at all.
Modern models can process whole sentences at once, unlike word-by-word RNNs.
RNNs are still faster than any modern model because they read one word at a time.
Only Bag of Words can be used for translation tasks.

exercises.explain: While older models like RNNs read text word-by-word (making them slow for long texts), newer models can process all words in a sentence at once. However, they all share the same goal: converting text into numbers to understand its meaning and predict what comes next.
